methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
The Nuclear Company is a Series A–stage nuclear energy startup founded by Jonathan Webb, Patrick Maloney, and Kiran Bhatraju, with $50M+ in total capital raised. The valuation exercise draws on a sector comp set of 8 private peers (including Fuse Energy, Oklo, TerraPower, X-Energy, and Kairos Power) and 3 public comparables (OKLO, NNE, SMR).

Stage-progression methodology, anchoring to the $50M raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement, implies a post-money valuation of $100M. Peer-median analysis was not applicable due to insufficient narrow-stage peers within nuclear energy; the available comp set lacked three or more same-stage or adjacent-stage datapoints with comparable valuation tiers, making sector-wide medians potentially misleading rather than informative.

The triangulated valuation range derived from applicable methods supports $85M–$115M, with a central estimate of $100M. This single-method foundation reflects a meaningful constraint: the relative scarcity of sufficiently granular comparables in the nuclear energy subsector limits confidence in cross-validation. Key uncertainties include the timing and scale of near-term capital deployment, regulatory pathway milestones, and technology-specific execution risk—factors not captured in stage-based mechanics alone. Investors should view the $100M midpoint as reasonable for Series A context while acknowledging material sensitivity to company-specific progress and sector liquidity events.
